category: non-thread-safe type used in parallel context
title: thread_local! RefCell<Option<PerceptualGrid>> initialised lazily inside a rayon par_chunks_mut closure — rebuild races are harmless but borrow_mut panics if the same thread re-enters
description: PERCEPTUAL_GRID is stored in a thread_local! RefCell. When `perceptual_constancy=true`, every pixel in a rayon row closure calls `PERCEPTUAL_GRID.with(|g| g.borrow_mut() ...)`. Because rayon workers are persistent threads the lazy init is actually fine, but RefCell's dynamic borrow checker panics on any re-entrant borrow. The `apply_tone_math` function is not marked non-re-entrant and nothing prevents a future wrapper from calling it while a borrow is held (e.g. via a panic unwind path that also calls it). Additionally, on the _current_ call shape the RefCell is borrowed, the closure calls `PerceptualGrid::new()` (which itself iterates and may panic), and the RefCell is held across that call — if PerceptualGrid::new ever panics the RefCell stays poisoned for that thread's lifetime.

title: Cancellation flag checked before rayon::join but not re-checked inside joined closures atomically — cancellation acknowledged late under load
description: encode_variants_cancellable checks `cancel.load(Ordering::Relaxed)` at three points (lines 116, 155, 169) before spawning the three-way rayon::join at line 185. Each rayon closure also has an early-out check (lines 187, 196, 203), but these checks are all relaxed loads with no fence. Under high parallelism the outer checks can pass and all three closures can start before a concurrent cancel store propagates. The real risk is that a cancel that arrives after the outer check and before the inner checks is silently ignored — the three encodes complete and their outputs are returned as a valid VariantSet rather than Err(Cancelled). This is a timing window where the caller's cancellation contract is violated.

category: unsafe Send impl without Sync — misuse risk with rayon
title: Decoder is Send but not Sync; decode_jxtc_region passes &Decoder to map_init — safe only because map_init gives each thread its own Option<Decoder>, but the pattern is fragile
description: Decoder wraps a raw `*mut ffi::JxlDecoder` and is declared `unsafe impl Send`. The `decode_jxtc_region` function uses `rayon::par_iter().map_init(|| Decoder::new(...), ...)` which correctly gives each Rayon thread its own `Option<Decoder>`, so no actual data race occurs today. However, the `unsafe impl Send` relies on the doc comment claim that 'libjxl context is looked up per-use, not stored'. If libjxl internally shares any per-process context (e.g. a global memory pool or LCMS context used without locking) the Send impl would be unsound. This is hard to verify from Rust code alone and represents a latent race if the underlying C library assumptions change. The same risk applies to Encoder in jxl_encode.rs.

title: rayon::join does not short-circuit on first Err — both sibling encodes always run to completion even after one cancels or errors
description: The nested rayon::join at lines 185-208 starts three encode tasks. If the first branch (thumb_300) returns Err(Cancelled) early, the other two branches (preview and full) are already executing in parallel and will not be interrupted — they run to full completion before the join unifies the results. For large images this means cancellation (or any error) in the thumb branch does not prevent the two larger encodes from consuming CPU for their full duration. The ? operator at line 209 surfaces the error correctly, but by then the work is already done. This is especially wasteful when cancel is set: all three encoders finish before the Cancelled error propagates.

title: decode_progressive_frames_borrowed leaks the JxlDecoder handle on JXL_DEC_ERROR or early-break paths
description: The free function `decode_progressive_frames_borrowed` creates a raw `*mut ffi::JxlDecoder` at line 813 and calls `ffi::JxlDecoderDestroy(dec)` only once at line 879 inside the `unsafe` block. However the function returns `None` at line 879 if status is JXL_DEC_ERROR or S_NEEDIN without a buffer. Looking at the code flow: on the `JXL_DEC_ERROR` break at line 874, the code falls through to line 879 where `JxlDecoderDestroy` IS called. On the `S_NEEDIN` break it also falls to 879. The final `return None` at line 895 (inside the else branch) is reached after JxlDecoderDestroy has already been called. This path is actually correct — the destroy always happens at line 879. However, the error path at line 858 (`status = ffi::JxlDecoderStatus::JXL_DEC_ERROR; break;`) inside the S_NEEDOUT branch breaks before reaching line 879's destroy only if that path is reached inside the if-let. Re-reading: all breaks lead to line 879 which always calls destroy. The real risk is the absence of a `JxlDecoderCloseInput` before the break paths — libjxl may hold references to the input slice, and destroying without closing may leave libjxl in undefined state for future creates in the same process.

category: unsafe raw pointer write in parallel closure
title: Raw pointer write `*cur_row_ptr.add(col)` inside sequential loop is safe by construction but relies on non-obvious lifetime and aliasing invariants
description: decompress_rows_into obtains a raw mutable pointer `cur_row_ptr` from `cur_row.as_mut_ptr()` at line 84 and writes through it at line 156 inside the per-column loop. This is safe because the loop is sequential and the split_at_mut at line 77 ensures `above` and `cur` do not alias. However the unsafe block is not marked or scoped to the minimum span — the pointer is kept live across the entire outer row loop. If this function were ever called from a parallel context (e.g. wrapping it in a par_iter over rows), the `out[..n].split_at_mut(row_base)` logic would re-borrow the same backing buffer from multiple threads, which is UB. There is currently no parallelism here, but the absence of documentation or a `#[allow(clippy::...)]` note means the invariant is invisible.

category: no cancellation propagation into rayon demosaic/pipeline kernels
title: Rayon-parallelised demosaic and pipeline passes have no cancellation token — long operations cannot be interrupted once started
description: All rayon par_chunks_mut loops in demosaic.rs and pipeline.rs iterate to completion with no way to abort early. For large images (e.g. 20 MP) these passes can take tens of milliseconds per stage. If a cancellation signal arrives (e.g. from casabio_encode's AtomicBool cancel, or from jxl_casadecoder's DecodeOptions.cancel) after these passes start, the caller must wait for them to finish before the error can be returned. Adding an optional AtomicBool check every N rows (e.g. every 64 rows) would allow sub-50ms cancellation granularity without measurable throughput impact.

category: no worker-pool abstraction for Decoder/Encoder reuse across threads
title: decode_jxtc_region uses rayon map_init with per-thread Decoder slot — pattern is not composable or reusable across call sites
description: The map_init closure creates a new Decoder per rayon thread per call to decode_jxtc_region. Each call discards and recreates these thread-local decoder slots, paying JxlDecoderCreate cost at every batch boundary. A thread-local decoder pool (analogous to the PerceptualGrid thread_local! pattern used elsewhere) would allow decoder reuse across successive decode_jxtc_region calls on the same thread without any synchronization cost. This is an opportunity rather than a bug — the current pattern is correct but suboptimal for repeated use.
